class_schedule.topic: Universal Design for Learning: Principles and Practice
learning_objectives: Explain the three principles of UDL and their neuroscience foundations
Distinguish between accommodation, modification, and universal design
Apply UDL to proactively design inclusive learning experiences
readings: CAST - Universal Design for Learning Guidelines 2.2
Rose, D. & Meyer, A. - Teaching Every Student in the Digital Age Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Universal Design for Learning is not about compliance. It is a design philosophy that creates flexible learning experiences benefiting all students, including those with disabilities, English learners, and students from diverse backgrounds.
key_concepts: Three UDL principles: engagement, representation, action/expression
Recognition, strategic, and affective brain networks
Accommodation (individual adjustment) vs. universal design (proactive flexibility)
Curb cut effect: designs for accessibility that benefit everyone
UDL as a framework for equity, not just disability
lecture_notes.demonstration: UDL audit: evaluate a course module against UDL guidelines and identify three improvements that would benefit all learners, not just those with disabilities

review.value: Curb cuts were designed for wheelchair users but benefit parents with strollers, travelers with luggage, and delivery workers with carts. Similarly, captions designed for deaf students help ESL students, commuters, and anyone in a noisy environment.
UDL designs flexibility into the learning experience from the start. Accommodations retrofit after a student discloses a disability. UDL is more efficient, equitable, and effective.

class_schedule.topic: Web Accessibility Standards: WCAG 2.1 and Section 508
learning_objectives: Apply WCAG 2.1 success criteria to evaluate digital content
Identify Section 508 requirements for educational institutions
Use automated and manual testing tools to audit accessibility

lecture_notes.introduction: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines provide the technical standards that translate accessibility principles into measurable, testable requirements. Section 508 makes these standards legally enforceable for federally funded institutions.
key_concepts: POUR principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust
WCAG conformance levels: A, AA, AAA
Key AA criteria: color contrast (4.5:1), keyboard navigation, alt text, captions, headings
Automated tools: axe, WAVE, Lighthouse — what they catch and what they miss
Manual testing: screen reader testing, keyboard-only navigation, cognitive walkthrough
lecture_notes.demonstration: Accessibility audit lab: audit a real educational website using both automated tools (WAVE) and manual testing (keyboard-only navigation, screen reader)

review.value: Automated tools catch about 30% of accessibility issues (color contrast, missing alt text, heading structure). The remaining 70% (meaningful alt text, keyboard trap, focus order, cognitive clarity) require manual testing.
Hundreds of universities have faced ADA/Section 508 lawsuits over inaccessible digital content. Proactive accessibility is both ethically correct and legally protective.

learning_objectives: Create accessible documents (Word, PDF, Slides) that meet WCAG standards
Produce accessible multimedia content including captions and audio descriptions
Design accessible assessments that measure learning without barriers

lecture_notes.introduction: Most accessibility barriers in education come from everyday content: documents without headings, images without alt text, videos without captions, PDFs that screen readers cannot parse. Fixing these common issues eliminates the majority of barriers.
key_concepts: Document accessibility: headings, lists, alt text, reading order, color usage
PDF accessibility: tagged PDFs, reading order, form labels
Video captioning: synchronized captions, accuracy standards (99%), speaker identification
Audio description: narrating visual information for blind/low-vision users
Accessible assessment: alternative formats, extended time design, universal test design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Remediation workshop: take an inaccessible Word document, PowerPoint, and PDF and make each fully accessible, testing with a screen reader

review.value: Screen reader users navigate by headings. A document without headings is like a book without a table of contents — the user must read linearly to find anything.
Auto-generated captions are 80-90% accurate. DCMP standards require 99% accuracy. That 10-19% error rate makes auto-captions unreliable for deaf students; human review is essential.

learning_objectives: Identify major categories of assistive technology for learning
Explain how screen readers, magnifiers, alternative input devices, and text-to-speech support diverse learners
Design educational technology that works with common assistive technologies
readings: Cook, A. & Polgar, J. - Assistive Technologies: Principles and Practice Ch. 1-4
ATIA - Introduction to Assistive Technology in Education

lecture_notes.introduction: Assistive technology bridges the gap between a learner's abilities and the demands of the learning environment. Educational technologists must understand these tools to design content that works WITH them, not against them.
key_concepts: Screen readers: JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver — how they parse web content
Screen magnification and zoom technologies
Alternative input: switch access, eye tracking, voice control
Text-to-speech and speech-to-text for learning disabilities
AT in the classroom: IEP/504 plan technology requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: AT experience lab: complete a learning module using only a screen reader, then using only keyboard navigation, then using voice control. Document barriers encountered.
setup_requirements: Screen reader software (VoiceOver built-in on Mac)
Learning module to navigate with AT

review.value: Screen readers linearize content and read in document order. Content that relies on spatial arrangement (side-by-side comparisons, multi-column layouts) may become meaningless when linearized.
The best way to ensure AT compatibility is to follow web standards: semantic HTML, ARIA roles, keyboard event handlers, focus management. Custom widgets that ignore standards break AT.

learning_objectives: Identify cognitive accessibility barriers in digital learning content
Design for learners with ADHD, dyslexia, autism, and intellectual disabilities
Apply plain language principles to instructional content
readings: Seeman, L. & Cooper, M. - Making Content Usable for People with Cognitive and Learning Disabilities

lecture_notes.introduction: Cognitive accessibility is the least understood and most overlooked dimension of accessible design. More students have cognitive and learning disabilities than physical or sensory disabilities, yet most accessibility efforts focus on the latter.
key_concepts: Cognitive load reduction: simplify, chunk, provide structure
Plain language: shorter sentences, active voice, common words
Predictable navigation and consistent layout
Error prevention and recovery for users with executive function challenges
Flexible timing: removing or extending time limits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Content simplification exercise: rewrite a complex educational text using plain language principles while maintaining accuracy and depth
setup_requirements: Hemingway Editor or similar readability tool

review.value: Plain language is not 'dumbing down.' It is removing unnecessary complexity so the reader can focus cognitive resources on understanding the content rather than parsing the language.

Consistent navigation, predictable layouts, and clear labels reduce the cognitive overhead of using a digital tool. For users with executive function challenges, every navigation decision is a potential barrier.

class_schedule.topic: Accessibility Program Management and Institutional Culture
learning_objectives: Design an institutional accessibility program for educational technology
Train faculty and staff on accessibility practices
Develop accessibility procurement policies for educational technology purchases
readings: Lazar, J. et al. - Ensuring Digital Accessibility Through Process and Policy Ch. 6-8

lecture_notes.introduction: Individual expertise in accessibility is necessary but not sufficient. Sustainable accessibility requires institutional policies, processes, procurement standards, and a culture that values inclusive design.
key_concepts: Accessibility governance: policy, training, monitoring, remediation
Procurement: requiring VPATs and accessibility testing before purchasing educational technology
Faculty training: building accessibility into course design workflows
Content remediation: prioritizing and scheduling the accessibility remediation of existing content
Accessibility culture: moving from compliance to commitment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Procurement evaluation: review three educational technology product VPATs (Voluntary Product Accessibility Templates) and recommend which product best meets accessibility requirements
setup_requirements: Sample VPATs from three educational technology products

review.value: A Voluntary Product Accessibility Template documents a product's conformance with accessibility standards. Institutions should require VPATs from all vendors and verify claims through independent testing.
Compliance-driven accessibility does the minimum required by law. Commitment-driven accessibility designs for inclusion because it is the right thing to do. The outcomes are dramatically different.

questions.question: An instructor creates a video lecture with no captions, arguing that 'none of my students are deaf.' Using UDL principles, explain why this reasoning is flawed and identify at least three student populations who benefit from captions.
questions.answer: This reasoning is flawed because UDL designs proactively for variability, not reactively for disclosed disabilities. Many students who benefit from captions do not identify as deaf. Populations: (1) Students who are hard of hearing (many do not disclose or may not have formal accommodations). (2) English language learners who process written text more effectively than spoken language. (3) Students studying in noisy environments (dorms, public transit, shared spaces). (4) Students with auditory processing disorders who struggle to parse speech but read fluently. (5) Students who learn better through multimodal input (reading while listening). Captions also improve content searchability and enable studying in sound-sensitive environments like libraries.

rubric.key: Explains why reactive (wait for disability disclosure) approach fails
Identifies at least three distinct benefiting populations
Connects to UDL's principle of multiple means of representation

questions.question: Automated accessibility tools report that a website has zero errors. Does this mean the website is accessible? Explain what automated tools can and cannot detect.
questions.answer: Zero automated errors does NOT mean the website is accessible. Automated tools can detect approximately 30% of WCAG issues: missing alt text (but not whether alt text is meaningful), color contrast ratios, missing form labels, and heading hierarchy violations. They CANNOT detect: whether alt text accurately describes the image, whether tab/focus order is logical, whether content is cognitively clear, whether custom interactive widgets work with screen readers, whether video captions are accurate, or whether the site is usable for people with cognitive disabilities. A comprehensive audit requires manual testing with assistive technologies, cognitive walkthroughs, and ideally, testing with users who have disabilities.

rubric.key: Correctly states zero automated errors does not mean accessible
Lists specific issues automated tools CAN detect
Lists specific issues that require manual testing

quizzes.topic: Assistive Technology, Cognitive Accessibility, and Program Management

questions.question: A screen reader user navigates an online course and encounters an interactive drag-and-drop quiz. Explain why this is likely inaccessible and describe how to redesign the quiz to be accessible while maintaining the same learning objective.
questions.answer: Drag-and-drop interactions typically require mouse-based spatial manipulation, which screen readers cannot perform (they read linearly and use keyboard commands). The visual spatial arrangement may convey meaning that is not communicated to the screen reader. Redesign: provide an equivalent keyboard-accessible interaction, such as a dropdown menu where the user selects the correct category for each item, or a sortable list that can be reordered using arrow keys. The learning objective (e.g., categorizing items or sequencing steps) can be assessed through either interface. Implement ARIA live regions to announce changes as items are moved. The drag-and-drop can remain as one option while the accessible alternative is always available.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies why drag-and-drop fails with screen readers
Proposes a specific accessible alternative that meets the same objective
Mentions ARIA or other technical accessibility implementations

questions.question: What is a VPAT and why should educational institutions require one before purchasing educational technology? What is the main limitation of relying solely on VPATs?
questions.answer: A VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) is a document in which a vendor self-reports their product's conformance with accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1, Section 508). Institutions should require VPATs because they provide a standardized way to evaluate and compare products' accessibility before investing money and locking into a platform that may exclude students with disabilities. The main limitation: VPATs are self-reported by the vendor, creating an incentive to overstate conformance. Vendors may report partial conformance as full, test only a subset of features, or use outdated criteria. Institutions should verify VPAT claims through independent testing with automated tools, manual evaluation, and ideally, user testing with people who use assistive technology.

rubric.key: Explains why institutions should require them
Identifies self-reporting as the key limitation and proposes verification

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Accessibility Audit and Remediation Plan
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive accessibility audit of a real educational technology product or online course. Use both automated tools and manual testing (screen reader, keyboard-only, cognitive walkthrough). Produce an audit report documenting all findings, prioritized by severity. Create a remediation plan with specific fixes, timeline, and resource estimates.
objectives: Conduct a rigorous accessibility audit using multiple methods
Prioritize accessibility issues by impact on learners
Create actionable remediation plans with specific technical fixes
Apply UDL principles beyond minimum WCAG compliance
deliverables: Accessibility audit report (3,000 words) with screenshots
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance checklist with pass/fail for each criterion
Prioritized issue list with severity ratings
Remediation plan with specific fixes, timeline, and effort estimates

criteria: Both automated and manual testing methods used
Testing with at least one assistive technology documented
WCAG criteria correctly mapped to each issue
Severity ratings are consistent and defensible
Specific, implementable fixes for each issue
Goes beyond minimum compliance to proactive inclusive design
Identifies opportunities for multiple means of representation, engagement, and expression
Actionable remediation plan with realistic estimates

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on accessibility standards, UDL, and assistive technology

lab_assignments.description: Weekly labs: automated testing, screen reader testing, document remediation, caption editing

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive accessibility audit and remediation plan

midterm_examination.description: Covers UDL, WCAG, document accessibility, and assistive technology

final_project.description: Institutional accessibility program design with policy, training, and procurement framework

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Assistive technology experience requires hands-on practice. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Audit assignments require access to products that may have time-limited trial periods.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review of audit reports is required. All audit work and remediation plans must be individually produced.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All audit findings must be from your own testing. Do not fabricate accessibility issues. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with screen reader software (NVDA for Windows, VoiceOver for Mac). WAVE browser extension. Access to at least one educational technology product for auditing.
